Toward a synchronous quantum-hybrid machine — Part 1: a NAND-only memory and the enable discipline
Don Gunter — WodansEye Research Series — Verified design note and prototype — Revision 2, July 22, 2026
Companions: “The Coherent NAND Execution Loop” (Part 2); “The Instruction Pointer Was Already There” (Part 3); “The Rauf Quantum Local Machine.”

Purpose and result
This note begins the slow, inspectable build of a small synchronous machine whose functional units will eventually be trained quantum circuits — the shells of the Rauf Quantum Local Machine work. It builds only the memory subsystem, classically, at the bit level, so that every wire can be checked by hand.
One rule governs it, and the rule is the paper:
The address bus does not carry data. It raises exactly one enable line. Address selects; separate read and write lines transfer.
That sounds like ordinary static-memory engineering, because it is. Its importance here is what happened afterward: the rule crossed into the quantum domain unchanged. In Part 2 a cycle position selects a location while the active status is the stored data; in Part 3 a one-hot micro-instruction pointer raises exactly one of six control words while the instruction word transfers separately. The discipline written for a classical decoder turned out to be the addressing discipline of the quantum sequencer as well.
Everything is assembled from a single primitive, NAND, because NAND is functionally complete and because a trained NAND shell already exists in the companion work. The classical netlist below is therefore a drop-in target: the wiring does not change when the primitive goes quantum.
Revision 2 adds a test suite covering every claimed property, including the two that carry the design — that the decoder is one-hot for every address, and that a write to one address disturbs no other cell.

Claim labels

• [BUILT] — implemented in nandmem.py, plain Python, no dependencies.

• [TESTED] — verified by the accompanying suite test_nandmem.py (eleven tests).

• [DESIGN] — a design decision or planned structure, not yet built.

• [OPEN] — an unresolved question deferred to a later part.

1. The one primitive
The whole memory is assembled from NAND, with NOT, AND, and the decoder derived from it.
def NAND(a, b): return 0 if (a and b) else 1
def NOT(a): return NAND(a, a)
def AND(a, b): return NOT(NAND(a, b))This matters beyond tidiness. In the companion Rauf Quantum Local Machine note, a NAND gate was trained as a two-qubit variational shell to a perfect truth table. Because the memory below is pure NAND, each gate in it is a place a trained shell can later stand, with no change to the surrounding architecture.
[BUILT] [TESTED]
2. The memory cell: a gated D-latch that waits
A memory cell is a gated D-latch built from four NAND gates. Its enable input E is the cell's word line, driven by the address decoder. When E is low the cell holds its stored bit; when E is high the cell captures the write bus. "NAND memory that waits" is literal: the latch holds — waits — until its address enables it.
class Cell:
def __init__(self):
self.Q, self.Qbar = 0, 1
def drive(self, D, E):
top = NAND(D, E) # E=0 -> top=1 (hold)
bot = NAND(top, E) # E=0 -> bot=1 (hold)
for _ in range(4): # settle the cross-coupled latch
self.Q = NAND(top, self.Qbar)
self.Qbar = NAND(bot, self.Q)
return self.QThree properties are tested rather than asserted: an enabled cell captures D; a disabled cell holds its value across repeated cycles even while the opposite value is presented on D; and Q and Qbar remain complementary. Four gates, one stored bit.
[BUILT] [TESTED]
3. The address decoder: enable, not transfer
A two-bit address feeds a 2-to-4 decoder that raises exactly one of four word lines. This is the physical meaning of the design rule: the address bus is converted into a one-hot set of enables and never touches the stored value.
def decode2to4(a1, a0):
return [
AND(NOT(a1), NOT(a0)), # addr 00 -> line 0
AND(NOT(a1), a0 ), # addr 01 -> line 1
AND( a1 , NOT(a0)), # addr 10 -> line 2
AND( a1 , a0 ), # addr 11 -> line 3
]The one-hot property is checked for all four addresses: exactly one line rises, and it is the correct one. In real static memory this is precisely what address lines do — they drive a decoder whose outputs are word lines, while bit lines carry data in and out. The separation is not an implementation detail; it is the reason address timing and data timing can be reasoned about independently, and it is what Parts 2 and 3 inherited.
[BUILT] [TESTED]
4. The memory device on the bus
The device ties the pieces together. On each access the address is decoded to a single enable; the write control gates that enable so only the addressed cell can latch; every other cell is driven with its own current state and therefore holds; and the enabled cell’s output is placed on the read bus.
class NandMemory:
def __init__(self):
self.cells = [Cell() for _ in range(4)] # 4 x 1-bit
def access(self, a1, a0, data_in, write):
enables = decode2to4(a1, a0) # address -> one enable high
out = 0
for i, cell in enumerate(self.cells):
E = AND(enables[i], write) # write only the enabled cell
q = cell.drive(data_in if E else cell.Q, E)
if enables[i]: # read: enabled cell -> read bus
out = q
return outThree buses meet here and stay distinct: the address bus into the decoder, the write bus as data_in, and the read bus as out. The control line write chooses transfer direction. The address participates in every access as an enable and in none as a value.
[BUILT]
5. Verification
The suite checks the following, all passing.
Read after write, for every pattern. All sixteen four-bit patterns are written across the four addresses and read back. The demonstration case:
WRITE addr 00 <- 1 READ addr 00 -> 1 OK
WRITE addr 01 <- 0 READ addr 01 -> 0 OK
WRITE addr 10 <- 1 READ addr 10 -> 1 OK
WRITE addr 11 <- 1 READ addr 11 -> 1 OKWrite isolation. Starting from all cells set, clearing exactly one address leaves the other three untouched — checked for each of the four addresses in turn. This is the enable discipline’s whole purpose, and it is the test that would fail first if the decoder or the write gating were wrong.
Non-destructive read. Repeated reads return the same values; reading does not disturb storage.
The write bus is ignored during a read. Presenting a 1 on data_in while write is low does not change a stored 1 to anything else, and does not leak into the cell. Address selects; data transfers only when the control line says so.
Primitive and decoder correctness. NAND, NOT, and AND match their truth tables; the decoder is one-hot and correct for all four addresses.
[TESTED]
6. The fetch cycle this plugs into
The memory above is one device on a synchronous bus. The near-term target was a classical fetch cycle of roughly five or six CPU micro-steps governed by one classical tick:

• CPU drives the program counter onto the address bus, raising one word line.

• Control is set to read; the enabled cell drives the read bus.

• CPU latches the read bit into its instruction or accumulator register.

• CPU decodes the operation.

• CPU executes — the ALU step, which is where a trained shell will later stand.

• The program counter advances, and the buses are rewritten for the next tick.
Because the bus is volatile transport — driven fresh each tick, with durable state living in the cells and CPU registers — the tick period is bounded only by the slowest device’s settling time, not by any cross-tick requirement.
A note on how this turned out. Part 3 built a six-word quantum microcode store, and its blocks map closely onto the six steps above: present/address, prepare, decode, execute, latch, advance. The correspondence is not surprising in itself — this is the standard fetch-execute cycle, and any careful decomposition lands near it. What is worth recording is that the classical outline transferred to the quantum sequencer with one substantive difference: the step 3 latch here captures the fetched operand, whereas Part 3’s fourth microstep latches the computed result. Both are legitimate; they are different latches at different points in the cycle. The outline anticipated the structure without anticipating that detail.
[DESIGN]
7. Why this is the right first brick
Two properties make this subsystem a foundation rather than a throwaway.
First, the address-as-enable discipline keeps address timing and data timing separable, which is what lets a later bus master govern devices by controlling enables without entangling that control with data transfer.
Second, the entire netlist is NAND. Each of the four gates in a cell, and each gate in the decoder, is a location where a trained quantum shell can later be placed. The classical machine built here is the skeleton the quantum functional units attach to, and the wiring is invariant across that substitution.
8. What Parts 2 and 3 did with this
8.1 The gate became reversible
Part 2 took the NAND primitive across the quantum boundary. The classical NAND discards its inputs; a coherent circuit cannot. The reversible form preserves both inputs and XORs the result into a target qubit, and is its own inverse — which is what makes the compute-copy-uncompute discipline possible, and therefore what makes a bounded coherent execution loop possible at all.
8.2 The enable discipline became the micro-instruction pointer
This is the strongest carry-over, and it was not planned.
In this note, an address raises exactly one word line out of four while the data rides separate lines. In Part 3, a one-hot micro-instruction pointer raises exactly one of six microcode words while the instruction word transfers separately. The same sentence describes both. The rule was written for a classical decoder and turned out to be how the quantum sequencer addresses its own control store.
8.3 But a memory cell and a program counter want opposite encodings
Part 3 found a distinction this note does not need but any successor will.
A memory cell holds: once written, it retains its bit until written again, and that persistence is the entire point. A program counter moves: exactly one position is current, and advancing must vacate the old one. In a classical circuit the difference is invisible, because clearing a bit is free. In a reversible circuit it is not — a controlled-NOT into the next position sets it while leaving the previous one set, producing a cumulative reachability record rather than a moving token. A genuine one-hot transition must clear its source explicitly.
Part 2’s active tape is the cumulative kind, and that is correct there, because the tape is meant to be an execution history. Part 3’s micro-instruction pointer is the moving kind, and that is correct there, because a pointer that accumulates is not a pointer. Both are one-hot in appearance; only one of them is one-hot in fact.
The generalization Part 3 states as a rule — encode by verb — begins here. This note’s cells hold, so they store cumulatively. A counter counts, so it should move.
8.4 A record has an interference cost
One result from Part 2 constrains any future quantum bus built on this skeleton. A retained record makes execution branches distinguishable, and distinguishable branches cannot interfere. A memory that remembers is, in quantum terms, a memory that decoheres what it remembers about. That is not a defect — it is the price of having a record at all — but it means a quantum memory bus must decide, per transaction, whether it wants a durable record or interferable amplitudes. It cannot have both.
Part 3 sharpens this into a property of encodings rather than of memories: the cumulative tape contributes which-path information, while the moving one-hot pointer does not, because it leaves no accumulated history behind. Encodings have interference costs, not only gate costs.
9. Open items
The first of these is now partly closed; the rest stand.

• The CPU device. Part 3 built a six-address micro
